title: Chapter 36: Together
content: The studio smelled like coffee and pine cleaner and the particular ozone tang of equipment that had been running since dawn. Not the Nest — nothing like the Nest. This was a room above ground, on the third floor of a repurposed warehouse in Culver City, with windows that actually opened. Actual windows. Cass had stood in front of them for a full two minutes before anyone arrived, watching the morning light pool on the hardwood floor like it had every right to be there.

The board was a SSL 4000 — vintage, warm, the kind of console that required actual hands on actual faders. Cass sat behind it now, rolling the chair forward until his knees bumped the underside. The engineer, a woman named Dara with silver rings on every finger and the unhurried calm of someone who'd recorded jazz legends and death metal frontmen with identical professional indifference, had already patched everything. She glanced at him once — just once — when he walked in. Everyone in the industry knew the story. The Phantom of the Deep Web. The trial. The woman in the butterfly dress. But Dara had worked with weirdos before, and Cass was hardly the strangest person to ever sit behind her board. She'd set the session up, checked the talkback, and retreated to the corner with a paperback, present if needed and invisible if not.

Cass pulled on the headphones. The padded cushions pressed against his ears, familiar as breathing. His fingers walked across the faders without thought — 1, 2, 3, testing the throw, feeling the resistance. Something in his shoulders released. A tension he'd carried so long he'd forgotten it was there, loosening like a fist uncurling. It didn't matter that this wasn't the Nest. It didn't matter that the room was wrong and the board was different and the light kept falling across his hands in ways that would have driven him insane six months ago. This — the faders, the board, the headphones — this was where he lived, regardless of the room.
He looked through the glass.
Aline stood at the mic.

She was wearing the black butterfly — not as disguise, not as costume, not as armor. As jewelry. A small enamel pin on the collar of a plain white T-shirt. No elaborate dress. No stage makeup. No persona. Her hair was pulled back in a messy knot, and she wore jeans and sneakers, and she looked — she looked like herself. Just herself. The microphone hung before her like a question, and she adjusted the pop filter with one hand, her fingers lingering on the metal mesh the way someone might touch a touchstone.
She looked through the glass at him.
He looked back.

And for a moment — one suspended, terrible, beautiful moment — the entire weight of their story hung between them. The surveillance. The cameras. The Nest with its black walls and its impossible acoustics and its phantom who built a cathedral of sound to hold one woman captive. The trial. The testimony. The articles and the hot takes and the internet's endless appetite for turning someone's worst chapter into content. The fear — his fear, her fear, the fear that lived in the spaces between them like a third person in the relationship, always listening, always watching.
Then Cass nodded.
Aline nodded back.

VERA's voice came through the speakers, warm and unhurried, monitoring levels from wherever she lived now — a server rack in a climate-controlled room two buildings over, close enough to be present, far enough to give them space. "Levels are good. Whenever you're ready."
Aline closed her eyes. Opened them. She leaned into the mic, and she sang.

Not a song about danger. Not a song about darkness or phantoms or the deep web or any of the words that had been stapled to their story like headlines. A love song. Simple and honest and devastating the way only a love song can be when the person singing it has survived everything that should have made love impossible.

*I was a room with no windows*
*And you were the light that walked in*
*Not to find me — I was already found*
*Just waiting for someone to look*

Her voice filled the booth. Older now — rougher at the edges, the silk of it worn to something more interesting, more confident. Not the frightened singer from The Hollow, wide-eyed and trembling on a stage she didn't understand. Not the viral sensation of "Butterfly Rising," automated and processed into someone else's idea of perfection. Not the legal witness or the internet's favorite drama or the woman in the butterfly dress who captivated a courtroom. She was Aline. A woman singing to a man she chose, in a room full of light, about what it feels like to be seen without being watched.

Cass's hands moved on instinct. He should have been programming. Should have been building the track in layers — kick, snare, baseline, each one quantized and compressed and perfect. That was how the Phantom worked. That was how the Nest worked. Every sound controlled, curated, corrected into flawlessness.
Instead, he stood up. Walked to the piano.

A Yamaha C3 grand, black lacquer gleaming under the studio lights. Dara looked up from her book. The talkback was still open — Aline could see him through the glass, could see him sit down on the bench, could see him place his fingers on the keys for the first time in three years.
He played.

The chord was wrong. His left hand came in a half-beat late, fingers stumbling over the transition from C minor to E-flat like they'd forgotten the geography of the keyboard. Three years of producing on pads and screens — three years of the Nest, where every sound was a digital construct and his hands never touched anything real — had left him rusty. The muscle memory was there, buried, but it surfaced slowly, imperfectly, a diver rising from deep water with the bends still crackling in their joints.

He missed a note in the second measure. Then another in the fourth. His right hand found the melody and lost it, found it again, the phrasing uneven in a way that would have made the Phantom twitch with obsessive correction.

Dara and the second engineer exchanged a glance. The second engineer — a younger guy named Tomás — raised an eyebrow. This was Cassian Mercer. The producer who built an empire on sonic perfection. And he was fumbling through a piano part like a conservatory student on their first jury.
But Aline kept singing.
And the mistakes — they were beautiful.

Not despite their imperfection. Because of it. Because each stumble was a heartbeat, each slightly late chord a breath, each fumbled transition the sound of a human hand on a real instrument in a real room. The Phantom's perfection was inhuman. Every note aligned to the grid, every frequency carved and polished until the music gleamed like a mirror with no reflection. Cassian Mercer's imperfection was art. It breathed. It bled. It was alive in the way that only flawed things can be alive.

*I stopped counting the cameras*
*When you looked at me and I believed it*
*Not the watching — the seeing*
*Not the holding — the letting go*

The piano and the voice found each other. Not perfectly. Not the way they would have in the Nest, where Cass could have aligned every syllable to every keystroke with surgical precision. They found each other the way two people do in a room — by listening, by adjusting, by being present. Aline's voice rose on "letting go," and Cass followed her, not because a program told him to but because he could hear where she was going and wanted to meet her there.

The first take ended. Aline pulled the headphones off one ear, looking through the glass. Her chest was rising and falling — really rising and falling, the breath of a woman who'd just sung her lungs out, not the controlled stage-breathing of a performer conserving energy. Cass could see the flush on her cheeks, the damp at her temples.
"That was —" she started through the talkback.

"One more," he said. Not because it wasn't good. Because he wanted to do it again. He wanted to play again. He wanted his hands on those keys, wanted the ache building in his forearms, wanted the physical reality of making music in a way the Nest had never once provided.

Second take. The piano intro was steadier now — still not perfect, still human, but his fingers remembered more of the map. The melody flowed, and Aline's voice flowed with it, and the room filled with sound the way a river fills a valley: not all at once, but inevitably, naturally, the only direction it could go.
Then the bridge. The high note.
Aline reached for it. And her voice cracked.

It wasn't a disaster — not a full break, not a collapse. Just a fracture, a hairline split in the tone where her vocal cords couldn't quite sustain the pitch. It lasted less than a second. In the Nest, Cass would have been on it before it finished happening — comping, tuning, smoothing the crack into seamless perfection until no one, not even Aline, would know it had occurred.
He didn't touch it.
He left it in.

The song continued. The crack sat there in the waveform like a scar, visible and real and honest, and Aline sang through it and past it and the notes on the other side were stronger for having survived it. When the last chord faded — Cass's left hand holding the bass note while his right lifted away from the keys, letting the string ring until it died — the silence that followed was the loudest thing in the room.
Then Cass pressed the talkback.
"Perfect."

Through the glass, Aline's face did something complicated. Her mouth opened and closed. Her eyes went bright. She pressed her palm flat against the studio glass, and he could see the faint fog of her breath bloom where her hand touched the surface. She was smiling — not the curated smile of a stage performer, not the thin press of someone managing their expression for an audience. A real smile. The kind that lives in the eyes before it ever reaches the mouth.
It was the first time he'd ever called anything perfect that wasn't digitally flawless.

They did a third take for safety, as Dara put it, her professional instincts overriding her sense of when to leave well enough alone. The third was good — cleaner, the piano more confident, Aline's voice navigating the high note with precision this time. But when Dara asked which take to comp from, Cass said, "The second. Use the second."
"The one with the crack?"
"The one with the crack."
Dara looked at him for a long moment. Then she nodded slowly, like she was hearing something she'd always known but never had the words for, and she marked Take 2 as the master.
After, they sat on the studio floor.

The session had run longer than planned. Three hours of recording, another hour of rough mixing while Aline dozed on the leather couch with one arm thrown over her eyes and Cass fiddled with levels he didn't need to fiddle with just because he could, just because his hands wanted to keep moving, just because stopping felt like it might let something in that he wasn't ready to face. His forearms ached. His shoulders burned. His fingers were stiff and complaining, the pads of them tender in a way that had nothing to do with pressing buttons and everything to do with pressing real keys, real ivory and real strings vibrating through real wood into real air.

He was exhausted. Genuinely, physically, bone-deep exhausted. The Nest had never made him tired. The Nest made everything mediated, digital, controlled — his body a passenger while his mind conducted an orchestra of pixels and algorithms. He could produce for eighteen hours straight in that black room and feel nothing but the faint buzz of too much coffee and too little sleep. But this — playing piano, producing in real time, listening with his whole body instead of a waveform monitor — this was raw and human and demanding and *tiring* in a way that felt like victory.

Aline had ordered takeout. Pad thai and green curry and spring rolls that came in little paper bags with the restaurant's logo — a laughing elephant — stamped in red ink on the side. She spread the containers between them on the floor, the sweet-sour smell of tamarind and lime filling the studio, cutting through the coffee-and-ozone atmosphere like sunlight through smoke. The taste of pad thai on his tongue — hot, bright, the crunch of peanuts and the soft give of rice noodles — was so sharply present that he almost laughed. When was the last time he'd tasted anything in a studio? In the Nest, he'd eaten at the console, barely registering the food, his attention always on the screens. Here, the flavors were almost aggressively real.

Aline sat cross-legged across from him, a container of green curry balanced on her knee, chopsticks in hand. She'd kicked off her sneakers. The black butterfly pin caught the light every time she moved — a small, steady glint against the white cotton of her T-shirt.

Cass leaned against the wall, chopsticks dangling from his fingers, head tipped back against the acoustic paneling. The ceiling was white. Ordinary. Not the black void of the Nest but just a ceiling, with a smoke detector and a light fixture and the faint watermark from a leak that had been fixed months ago.
"Is this what normal feels like?" he said.

Aline looked up from her curry. Her mouth was full — actually full, a piece of pepper dangling from her chopsticks — and she chewed and swallowed before answering, which was the most un-stage-managed thing he'd ever seen her do.
"No," she said. "Normal doesn't feel this good."

He looked at her. This woman. This woman who had walked into a phantom's lair — into *his* lair, The Hollow's lair, the Nest's lair, whatever name you gave the darkness he'd built around her like a second skin — and walked out still standing. Not unchanged. Not unbroken. But standing. And beside her, somehow, impossibly, was him. Not the Phantom. Not The Hollow. Cass. Just Cass. Tired and imperfect and alive in a way he hadn't been in years.
He put down the chopsticks. They clattered on the hardwood, and the sound was not quantized, not corrected, not processed. It was wooden sticks on wooden floor, and it was exactly what it was.
He reached across the space between them and took her hand.

Her fingers were warm. Sticky, actually — curry sauce on her thumb, a grain of rice near her knuckle. She hadn't wiped them. She hadn't performed the gesture of being clean and elegant before letting him touch her. She was just — present. Human. Imperfect.

He lifted her hand to his lips. Kissed her knuckles — gently, softly, the brush of his mouth against her skin light enough to be a question rather than a claim. A promise rather than a possession. The Phantom would have gripped. The Phantom would have held. The Phantom would have turned the gesture into a cage made of lips and fingers and the suffocating weight of someone who mistook ownership for love.
Cass just kissed. And let go.
"Thank you," he said, "for not being afraid of me."

The words came out rough. He wasn't looking at the window or the ceiling or the board or anywhere that might give him an escape route. He was looking at her. At this woman who had every reason to fear him — every reason to run, to rebuild her life on the opposite coast, to never step into a studio with him again — and who had instead stood at a microphone in a white T-shirt and sung a love song through a cracked high note like it was the most natural thing in the world.

Aline's eyes held his. Dark and steady and full of something that had nothing to do with the camera-ready intensity the internet had fallen in love with and everything to do with the simple, terrifying act of letting another person see you as you actually are.
"Thank you," she said, "for giving me something worth being brave for."

The light through the window shifted. Afternoon now — the sun had moved while they weren't watching, the way it does when you're inside something true. The studio smelled like coffee and curry and the fading warmth of a day that was almost over, and the piano sat in the corner with its lid still open, the strings humming faintly with the ghost of a song that wasn't perfect.
It was better than perfect.
It was real.

And outside the window, the sky went on — vast and ordinary and full of light — and they sat on the floor of a room above ground, holding hands like two people who had finally understood that love is not about control. It's not about walls or cameras or the careful architecture of keeping someone close.
It's about choosing to be seen. Cracked notes and sticky fingers and all.
